questions.promptText: Which collar shows a higher gorge height — the seam placed closer to the base of the neck?
questions.explanation: A higher gorge raises the visual line and lengthens the proportion. The seam sits close to the base of the neck.

questions.promptText: Which coat shows stronger waist suppression — more nipped in at the midsection?
questions.explanation: Waist suppression creates the X-silhouette. The stronger nip at the waist makes the chest and skirt read larger by contrast.

questions.promptText: Which jacket shows drape cut — soft vertical fullness over the chest, with a close-cut waist?
questions.explanation: Drape cut carries deliberate fullness over the chest that creates a soft vertical ripple, contrasted with a nipped waist.

questions.promptText: Which shoulder seam shows the roped shoulder — a small raised roll at the sleeve head?
questions.explanation: The roped shoulder (con rollino) sets the sleevehead with a raised roll, squaring the shoulder line. The soft shoulder slopes gently — the Neapolitan alternative.

questions.promptText: Which armhole position allows the arm to lift without the coat body moving — the tell of the high, closed armhole?
questions.explanation: A high, closed armhole lets the arm move while the coat body stays still. The clean gap at the side seam is the tell.

questions.promptText: Which coat skirt flares from the waist in an A-line — the lower body opening outward toward the hem?
questions.explanation: The A-line coat skirt opens below the suppressed waist, completing the hourglass silhouette and allowing the coat to clear the thighs when seated.

questions.promptText: Which collar removes the lapel entirely, framing the neck with a clean standing band?
questions.explanation: The stand collar (funnel, mandarin, or Nehru) removes the lapel — the busiest element of a coat — leaving only line. Less information, more form.

questions.promptText: Which jacket silhouette shows the sack cut — straight-hanging with no waist shaping?
questions.explanation: The sack cut has no darts, no suppression — cloth falls straight from shoulder to hem. A named alternative to the sculpted silhouette.

questions.promptText: Which wall surface shows béton brut — concrete left as cast, with the formwork grain visible?
questions.explanation: Béton brut means 'raw concrete' — left as cast from the formwork, board grain and all. The marks are a record of making.

questions.promptText: Which plan reads the walls as solid black mass — with rooms as carved white voids?
questions.explanation: Poché fills the wall solid so the rooms read as shaped voids rather than leftover space. The solid is the figure; the room is the ground.

questions.promptText: Which plan shows enfilade — doorways aligned so you see straight through a sequence of rooms?
questions.explanation: Enfilade aligns all the doorways on a single axis, so a standing view extends through five or more rooms in one vista.

questions.promptText: Which composition uses the gap between two elements as the focal point — the charged interval held between them?
questions.explanation: Ma is the deliberate negative space, the interval. The gap between elements is composed as carefully as the elements themselves.

questions.promptText: Which building lifts its mass off the ground on slim columns, leaving the ground level open?
questions.explanation: Pilotis raise the building's mass on columns, freeing the ground to flow underneath and making the heavy body appear to float.

questions.promptText: Which detail expresses the structure's joint — celebrating the connection rather than hiding it?
questions.explanation: Tectonic design expresses the frame and joints — the steel node, the timber connection — rather than encasing them. Structure becomes ornament.

questions.promptText: Which bowl reads as wabi-sabi — beauty through imperfection, the repair visible and valued?
questions.explanation: Wabi-sabi finds beauty in imperfection and impermanence. The repaired crack (kintsugi), the worn edge — these mark the object as real and lived-in.

questions.promptText: Which room reveals more the longer you look — quiet on first glance, deepening on attention?
questions.explanation: Shibui is understated beauty that rewards sustained attention. A simple surface with complex depth — the opposite of immediate visual loudness.

questions.promptText: Which surface shows truth to materials — the wood looking like wood, the concrete like concrete?
questions.explanation: Truth to materials means each material looks and behaves like itself — never veneered, printed, or faked to imitate another.

questions.promptText: Which object shows patina — the surface change earned through age and use?
questions.explanation: Patina is the surface character a material earns over time: the sheen on worn brass, the depth on oiled wood. It marks an object as quality.

questions.promptText: Which room shows biophilic design — a full nature-connection system of light, material, and sightline?
questions.explanation: Biophilic design is more than plants — it's daylight quality, natural materials, sightlines to green, and organic forms working as a system.

questions.promptText: Which interior shows japandi — Japanese restraint and asymmetry blended with Nordic warmth and natural material?
questions.explanation: Japandi combines Japanese minimalism (restraint, asymmetry, reverence for material) with Scandinavian warmth (light wood, comfort). The blend avoids both cold severity and clutter.
